slides.headline: We've handled 9 briefs in 90 days.
slides.eyebrow: Education · What music supervisors actually want — from 9 real briefs
fit_warnings: [01 hook.eyebrow] copy (69 chars) exceeds slot budget (48 at 44px) — needs a rewrite

slides.body: Here's what every music supervisor actually wanted — from their words, not ours. ↓

"Rights-cleared tracks with stems."
"Quick turnaround."
"African sounds for diverse casting."
"Real African music, not fusion."

Every brief. Every time. — — — WHAT THEY ACTUALLY MEAN "Rights-cleared" means one-stop.
One conversation. One deal. No separate master/sync clearance.
If you can't confirm one-stop, you're off the shortlist before the brief goes out. "Quick turnaround" means 24–48 hours, not 72.
The 11-hour turnaround in Month 2 got us the next three briefs.
Supervisors remember speed. "African sounds" means specific, not continental.
Afrobeats is not the same as Highlife.
Amapiano is not the same as South African jazz.
Supervisors doing their homework know the difference. Assume they do. — — — WHAT THEY NEVER SAY BUT ALWAYS NEED Stems in the delivery.
Not "available on request." In the initial folder. Complete metadata.
If your track arrives without BPM, key, and mood tags, it adds friction.
Friction means a different track gets selected. A clear fee position.
"What's your rate?" answered before it's asked.
Supervisors who have to chase a fee quote move on. — — — WHAT THEY REMEMBER Music quality gets you the shortlist.
Documentation quality gets you the placement. Three months of briefs confirmed this:
No rejected submission was rejected because of music quality.
Every rejection was documentation, rights, or turnaround. — — — HOW TO SPEAK THEIR LANGUAGE Submit — don't pitch.
One email: WAV master + stems + metadata card + rate card + rights confirmation. Not five messages. Not "I'll send the stems tomorrow." The supervisors who came back for Month 2 briefs came back because the first submission required zero follow-up. #SyncMaster #MusicSupervisor #AfricanComposers #SyncLicensing
